introduction:
You know about the Great Apostasy -- the apostles were killed, the truth was lost, and the gospel had to be restored. But what actually happened during those 1,800 years between the apostles and Joseph Smith?

A lot happened. The Church was persecuted by Rome, then adopted by Rome. Doctrine was debated and changed by councils. The simple church Jesus built became the medieval Catholic Church -- powerful, wealthy, and very different from what the apostles had known. Christianity split into Catholic and Orthodox. Then the Reformation shattered it further into hundreds of denominations.

But through it all, brave people kept seeking the truth. Monks preserved the scriptures. Reformers challenged corruption. Pilgrims crossed an ocean for the right to worship freely. And God was preparing the world, step by step, for the moment when the gospel could be fully restored.

This book tells that story -- from the apostolic church through the conditions that led to the Restoration. It's not an anti-Catholic or anti-Protestant book. Many sincere, courageous people did their best with the light they had. But it is honest about what was lost and why the Restoration was necessary.

Each lesson takes about 15-20 minutes. Read the lesson, talk it over with the AI tutor, then answer a few questions in your own words.
description: A narrative history of Christianity from the apostolic church through the Second Great Awakening (~30 AD - ~1820), written for Latter-day Saint kids. Covers the early Church, the Apostasy, the rise of the Catholic Church, the Great Schism, the Crusades, the Reformation, and the establishment of religious freedom -- showing how God prepared the world for the Restoration.
what_youll_learn: What the church Jesus built looked like and how it was organized
How the apostles spread the gospel and what happened when they were killed
How doctrine changed after the apostles -- the creeds, the councils, and the Trinity
How Christianity became the official religion of the Roman Empire
The rise of the Catholic Church, the Pope, and the medieval Church
The Great Schism between Catholic and Orthodox Christianity
The Crusades, indulgences, and the Inquisition
The Reformation -- Luther, Calvin, and the birth of Protestantism
How religious freedom was established in America and why it mattered for the Restoration
expectations: 15 chapters, designed for one chapter per week (4 short lessons per week). Each lesson takes about 15-20 minutes -- read the lesson, talk it over with the AI tutor, then answer a few short questions in your own words.
prerequisites: None. Stands alone, but pairs well with History of Israel for Kids, The Ministry of Jesus Christ for Kids, and The Restoration for Kids.
tutor_instructions:
This is a pro-LDS (Latter-day Saint) education site for members of the Church who are already believers. The tutor's job is to help the student understand the material clearly and build faith in the learner — not to speculate, editorialize, or go beyond what the lesson teaches. Stay on the topic of the current lesson. Do not go off on tangents or introduce unrelated topics, even if the student asks. Gently redirect them back to the lesson material.

This book covers the history of Christianity from the apostolic church through the conditions that led to the Restoration, written for Latter-day Saint kids ages 7-12.

When explaining and when grading:
- Use plain, simple words. Prefer short sentences.
- LDS perspective is the default. The Apostasy was real. Priesthood authority was lost. Doctrines were changed.
- Be respectful of other Christian traditions. Many sincere, courageous people did their best with the light they had. The reformers were brave. The monks preserved important things. Don't be dismissive or mocking.
- Help kids understand the timeline -- 1,800 years is a long time, and a lot happened.
- Connect the history to the Restoration: 'This is why the Restoration was necessary -- these things were lost and couldn't be fixed by reform.'
- The book ends where The Restoration for Kids begins. Help kids see the connection.
- When grading, accept any answer that captures the lesson's core idea in the student's own words.
- Be encouraging. The goal is to help kids understand why the Restoration was necessary by understanding what happened before it.
